concepts.lesson: Our atmosphere is a mixture of many gases. Nitrogen (78%) and Oxygen (21%) are the two gases which make up the bulk of the atmosphere. Carbon dioxide, argon, helium, ozone, and hydrogen are found in lesser quantities. Nitrogen is essential for plants, while oxygen is vital for humans and animals. Carbon dioxide helps in keeping the earth warm but its increase leads to global warming.
concepts.real_example: When we breathe in, we take in oxygen, which is the second most plentiful gas in the air.
key_points: Nitrogen (78%) and Oxygen (21%) are major gases
Carbon dioxide creates the Green House effect
Dust particles are also present in the air

concepts.lesson: The atmosphere is divided into five layers starting from the earth's surface: Troposphere (where we breathe and weather occurs), Stratosphere (contains ozone layer, ideal for flying airplanes), Mesosphere (where meteors burn up), Thermosphere (helps in radio transmission), and Exosphere (the uppermost layer with very thin air).
concepts.real_example: Commercial airplanes usually fly in the lower part of the Stratosphere to avoid weather disturbances found in the Troposphere.
key_points: Five distinct layers based on height
Troposphere is the most important layer
Ozone layer in the Stratosphere protects us from UV rays

concepts.lesson: Weather is the hour-to-hour, day-to-day condition of the atmosphere. It can change dramatically within a short period. Climate, on the other hand, is the average weather condition of a place for a long period of time (e.g., 25 years). This is why we can predict that a desert will be generally hot and dry.
concepts.real_example: A sudden rain shower in the afternoon is a weather change, but a hot tropical summer is part of the climate.
key_points: Weather is short-term
Climate is the long-term average
Both affect our daily lives and clothing

concepts.lesson: Temperature is the degree of hotness or coldness of the air. It changes not only between day and night but also from season to season. An important factor that influences the distribution of temperature is insolation. Insolation is the incoming solar energy intercepted by the earth. It decreases from the equator towards the poles.
concepts.real_example: The reason poles are covered with snow is because they receive very low insolation compared to the equator.
key_points: Temperature measured by Thermometer
Insolation affects temperature distribution
Equator is hotter than the poles

concepts.lesson: Air pressure is defined as the pressure exerted by the weight of air on the earth's surface. As we go up the layers of atmosphere, the pressure falls rapidly. Air always moves from high-pressure areas to low-pressure areas. Low pressure is associated with cloudy skies and wet weather, while high pressure is associated with clear and sunny skies.
concepts.real_example: Astronauts wear special protective space suits because there is no air pressure in space, which could cause their blood vessels to burst.
key_points: Pressure decreases with height
Horizontally, pressure is influenced by temperature
Air moves from High Pressure to Low Pressure

concepts.lesson: The movement of air from high-pressure area to low-pressure areas is called wind. Winds are broadly divided into three types: Permanent winds (Trade winds), Seasonal winds (Monsoon), and Local winds (Loo or Sea breeze). Wind is named after the direction from which it blows (e.g., a Westerly blows from the West).
concepts.real_example: The 'Loo' is a hot and dry local wind that blows in the northern plains of India during summer.
key_points: Winds move based on pressure differences
Permanent, Seasonal, and Local categories
Anemometer measures wind speed

concepts.lesson: When water evaporates from land and different water bodies, it becomes water vapor. Moisture in the air at any time is known as humidity. When water vapor rises, it cools and condenses to form clouds. When these droplets become too heavy to float, they come down as precipitation. Rain is the most common form of precipitation.
concepts.real_example: On a humid day, our sweat does not evaporate easily, making us feel very uncomfortable.
key_points: Humidity is water vapor in the air
Condensation forms clouds
Types of rainfall: Convectional, Orographic, and Cyclonic

concepts.lesson: Carbon dioxide released in the atmosphere creates a greenhouse effect by trapping the heat radiated from the earth. When its level increases due to factory smoke or car fumes, the heat retained increases the temperature of the earth. This is called global warming. This causes snow in coldest parts of the world to melt, leading to a rise in sea levels.
concepts.real_example: Increased flooding in coastal cities is a direct consequence of rising sea levels caused by global warming.
key_points: Caused by excessive Greenhouse gases
Leads to rise in Earth's average temperature
Can cause extinction of some plants and animals
